title: JSX expressions and attributes

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific React: Component-Based Browser Applications draft for JSX expressions and attributes; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: React Learn and Reference; React 19.2; provisions applicable to JSX expressions and attributes
sources.claim: JSX creates React element descriptions, embeds JavaScript expressions, and uses JavaScript property names and explicit escaping.

canonicalExplanation.summary: JSX creates React element descriptions, embeds JavaScript expressions, and uses JavaScript property names and explicit escaping.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A label uses htmlFor and a dynamic value is escaped when rendered as text. Your goal is to write and debug JSX with dynamic content, attributes and safe text interpolation. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Declarative UI and re-rendering; html semantics; Function components; javascript collections objects. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: JSX creates React element descriptions, embeds JavaScript expressions, and uses JavaScript property names and explicit escaping. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: It is not HTML, adjacent nodes need one parent, lowercase names mean host elements, and objects are not directly renderable children. This draft therefore rejects: JSX strings inserted through braces are interpreted as raw HTML.

includes: Embed expressions, use React DOM property names and keep data distinct from executable markup.
Write and debug JSX with dynamic content, attributes and safe text interpolation.
excludes: It is not HTML, adjacent nodes need one parent, lowercase names mean host elements, and objects are not directly renderable children.

outcomes: Write and debug JSX with dynamic content, attributes and safe text interpolation.

examples.title: JSX expressions and attributes: concrete case
examples.situation: A label uses htmlFor and a dynamic value is escaped when rendered as text.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the exact artifact and starting state: A label uses htmlFor and a dynamic value is escaped when rendered as text.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: React escapes ordinary JSX text expressions; raw HTML requires an explicit dangerous API and trusted sanitization.
examples.result: The result follows because React escapes ordinary JSX text expressions; raw HTML requires an explicit dangerous API and trusted sanitization.

Step 3 — Reject the misconception: JSX strings inserted through braces are interpreted as raw HTML.
examples.result: The boundary requires this correction: React escapes ordinary JSX text expressions; raw HTML requires an explicit dangerous API and trusted sanitization.

analogy.example: A state-driven component workshop can model JSX expressions and attributes, where inputs and state produce a UI description.

limitations: A workshop does not reproduce reconciliation identity, hook ordering, concurrent rendering, effects, or browser accessibility APIs.

recap.summary: You should now be able to write and debug JSX with dynamic content, attributes and safe text interpolation. Keep this boundary in view: It is not HTML, adjacent nodes need one parent, lowercase names mean host elements, and objects are not directly renderable children.
